title: The connection between pelvic floor tension and trauma
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

htmlContent:
Your body is not betraying you. It's protecting you.

I remember the first time a therapist asked me about my pelvic floor. I was lying on a yoga mat, trying to breathe into my lower belly, and she said, "Notice what happens right there, between your sit bones." I froze. My jaw clenched. I wanted to run out of the room. Here's the thing: I had no idea that part of my body even existed in a way that could hold tension. I thought pelvic floor issues were for postpartum moms or people with chronic pain conditions. Not for me. Not for someone whose trauma lived mostly in her head.

But that's exactly the problem. We think trauma lives in our heads. We think it's a story we tell ourselves, a memory we can't shake, a nightmare that visits at 3 a.m. And it is those things. But it's also a physical reality. It's a clenching in your jaw when you're scared. It's a knot in your stomach when you're anxious. And for so many of us, it's a pelvic floor that holds on like it's the last life raft on a sinking ship.

I've spent years working with my own nervous system, reading the research, and sitting with the discomfort. What I've learned is this: the pelvic floor is not just a muscle group. It's a crossroads. It's where your breath, your movement, your fear, and your safety all meet. And when trauma enters the picture, that crossroads becomes a fortress.

Stay with me here. I know this can feel strange to talk about. But I promise you, if you've ever felt that vague tightness in your hips, that strange holding in your lower belly, that sense that something down there just won't let go, you're not alone. And there's a reason for it.

The science of holding on

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, spent decades mapping how our nervous system responds to threat. His work shows that when we perceive danger, our body shifts into a state of protection. The vagus nerve, that long highway of communication between brain and body, sends signals to tighten, to brace, to prepare for impact. And guess what? The pelvic floor is one of the primary places that tightening lands.

Think about it. When you're scared, what do you do? You pull your legs together. You cross your thighs. You clench your glutes. You hold your breath. That's not a choice you're making. That's your nervous system saying, "Protect the soft parts. Guard the vulnerable places. Don't let anything in." And the pelvic floor, that sling of muscles at the base of your torso, is the gatekeeper.

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about trauma as incomplete biological responses. When we're threatened, our body prepares to fight, flee, or freeze. But if we can't complete that response, the energy gets stuck. It stays in the muscles, in the tissues, in the nervous system. And for many of us, especially those who experienced sexual trauma, medical trauma, or chronic stress, that stuck energy settles right into the pelvic floor.

I've worked with clients who describe it as a constant clenching, a feeling of being braced for impact even when they're safe in their own bed. They try to relax, but the muscles don't listen. They try to breathe, but the breath stops at the belly. They try to let go, but the body says no.

That's not weakness. That's survival. Your pelvic floor has been doing its job for years, maybe decades, keeping you safe from a threat that's long gone but still feels present. It's not broken. It's just stuck in a loop. And that loop can be unwound.

What the pelvic floor actually does

Let's get practical for a moment. The pelvic floor is a group of muscles that form a hammock at the bottom of your pelvis. It supports your bladder, your bowel, and your reproductive organs. It helps with continence, with sexual function, with core stability. It's supposed to contract and release, contract and release, like a gentle wave. But when trauma is present, that wave turns into a constant contraction.

Here's what that feels like in real life. Maybe you have trouble with constipation or urgency. Maybe you feel pain during sex or during a pelvic exam. Maybe you hold your breath without realizing it. Maybe you have a hard time feeling pleasure in your body because pleasure requires relaxation, and relaxation feels like letting your guard down. Right? I've heard that from so many people. "I can't let go. It feels dangerous."

That's your nervous system doing its job. But the problem is, your nervous system doesn't know the threat is over. It's still operating from an old map. And the only way to update that map is to work with the body directly.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of The Body Keeps the Score (paid link), wrote that trauma is not just an event that happened in the past. It's the residue of that event in the body. And that residue shows up in the pelvic floor for so many of us because the pelvic floor is intimately connected to our sense of safety, to our sexuality, to our ability to be vulnerable. A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score because it names this connection clearly and gives you language for what you're experiencing.

But naming it is only the first step. The next step is learning how to release it.

Releasing the fortress, gently

I want to be careful here. I'm not going to tell you to just "breathe into your pelvis" and expect everything to resolve. That kind of advice can actually make things worse, especially if you're not ready. If your pelvic floor has been clenched for years as a protective strategy, telling it to relax is like telling a guard dog to stop barking when the intruder is still in the room. The dog doesn't trust you. It needs to know it's safe first.

So where do you start? You start with your breath, but not in a forceful way. You start by simply noticing where your breath goes. Does it stop at your chest? Does it drop into your belly? Can you feel any movement in your lower ribs or your lower back? That's it. Just noticing. No forcing.

You can also start with gentle movement. Rocking your hips side to side. Making small circles with your pelvis. Letting your knees fall open while you lie on your back. These movements send a signal to your nervous system that you're not in immediate danger. They say, "We can move. We're not frozen."

Deb Dana, a clinician who specializes in Polyvagal Theory, talks about "glimmers" as small moments of safety that help regulate the nervous system. A glimmer might be the feeling of warm water on your skin. It might be the sound of a bird outside your window. It might be the sensation of your feet on the ground. And over time, these glimmers can help your nervous system learn that it's safe to release.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. He walks through how the body holds trauma and how we can gently work with it without re-traumatizing ourselves. It's not a quick fix, but it's a compassionate roadmap.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. It goes deeper into the idea of incomplete biological responses and how to complete them safely. I've found it helpful for understanding why my body does what it does, even when my mind doesn't understand.

And if you want a practical guide to working with your nervous system day to day, Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana is full of simple exercises that help you build a sense of safety in your body. It's not about fixing anything. It's about gently retraining your nervous system to know that you're okay now.

The hard truth about healing

I'm not going to lie to you. Releasing pelvic floor tension that's rooted in trauma is not a linear process. Some days you'll feel a release, a softening, a sense of space in your body. And other days you'll feel like you're back at square one, clenched and guarded and scared. That's normal. That's the nature of healing.

What matters is that you keep showing up with curiosity instead of judgment. When you notice the clenching, instead of saying "Why can't I relax?" try saying "Oh, there you are. I see you're trying to protect me. Thank you. I'm safe now." It sounds simple, but it changes the relationship between you and your body. You stop fighting your body and start partnering with it.

I've also learned that working with a pelvic floor physical therapist who understands trauma can be life-changing. These specialists know how to release tension without triggering you. They understand that the pelvic floor is not just a mechanical issue. It's an emotional and nervous system issue. If you have access to that kind of care, I encourage you to explore it.

And if you don't, you can still start where you are. You can place a hand on your lower belly and breathe. You can notice the sensations in your hips without trying to change them. You can let your knees fall open while you lie in bed. You can give your body permission to be exactly where it is, right now, without forcing anything.

That's not weakness. That's courage. That's the slow, patient work of reclaiming a part of yourself that's been holding on for too long. And you're not alone in it.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's essential book on how trauma lives in the body
  • Anchored (paid link) - Deb Dana's practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine's deep exploration of how the body releases trauma
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - Peter Levine's classic work on healing trauma through the body
